mainEntity.name: Why does every morning is a war zone. it starts the moment my daughter opens her eyes — sometimes before, in the middle of the night, when she comes to our room crying about tomorrow. 'i can't go. please don't make me. my stomach hurts. i feel sick. i'll do anything.' and she means it. the tears aren't fake. the hyperventilating is real. the physical symptoms — the stomachaches, the headaches, the nausea — seem so genuine i've taken her to the doctor three times this month alone. nothing's wrong, they say. it's anxiety, they say. as if knowing what it is makes it easier to watch your child suffer. as if a name for the monster makes it less terrifying. getting her dressed is a negotiation that takes three times longer than it should. getting her in the car requires promises i shouldn't make and threats i don't want to follow through on. getting her out of the car at school drop-off is the worst part. twenty minutes of pleading, bargaining, reasoning, and eventually prying her fingers off my arm while she sobs and the other parents watch. some look sympathetic. others look annoyed — we're blocking the drop-off lane. a few look judgmental, like i'm failing at something that should be simple. i've cried in that parking lot more times than i can count. i've sat in my car after finally getting her inside, shaking and exhausted before my workday has even begun. i've been late to work so often i'm worried about my job. my boss has made comments. i can't explain that my eight-year-old's anxiety is derailing my career. and here's the thing that makes me feel crazy: by pickup, she's usually fine. 'how was school?' i ask, bracing for trauma. 'it was okay,' she says. sometimes even good. the teachers say she participates, has friends, laughs at lunch, does her work. they don't see the monster. they see a normal kid. so why is every single morning like this? why does the anticipation of school create more suffering than school itself? i've tried everything i can think of. rewards for going without crying — she wants the reward, but the anxiety is stronger. consequences for the morning battles — punishment doesn't cure fear. ignoring the symptoms — she just escalates. accommodating by letting her stay home — then it's harder the next day. nothing works consistently. i know she's not being dramatic. i know she's not trying to manipulate me. i can see the genuine terror in her eyes. her body is flooding with stress hormones every morning, and she can't control it any more than she could control a fever. i just don't know how to help her. i don't know how to make her brain believe that school is safe when her nervous system is screaming that it's dangerous. i need tools. strategies. something concrete we can use. something that makes mornings survivable for both of us. something that helps her believe she can do this — because right now, she doesn't believe it, and i'm starting to lose faith too?

acceptedAnswer.text: SCHOOL ANXIETY / SCHOOL REFUSAL BEHAVIOR / SEPARATION ANXIETY IN ACADEMIC SETTINGS / ANTICIPATORY ANXIETY / SOCIAL ANXIETY IN SCHOOL / ACADEMIC PERFORMANCE ANXIETY! School anxiety encompasses a range of anxiety-related difficulties that interfere with school attendance, participation, or wellbeing. It may present as separation anxiety (fear of being away from parents/home), social anxiety (fear of peer judgment, social situations), academic anxiety (fear of failure, tests, performance), or generalized anxiety that attaches to the school environment. School refusal behavior — difficulty attending school associated with emotional distress — exists on a spectrum from morning resistance with eventual attendance to complete school avoidance. Key features include anticipatory anxiety (distress about upcoming school before it happens), physical symptoms (stomachaches, headaches, nausea with no medical cause), and the paradox that anxiety about school often exceeds actual distress during school. Without intervention, school anxiety can escalate, leading to chronic absenteeism, academic difficulties, social isolation, and reinforcement of avoidance patterns. Evidence-based intervention involves graduated exposure, coping skill development, parent coaching, school collaboration, and sometimes professional mental health support.

mainEntity.name: What is School Anxiety / School Refusal Behavior / Separation Anxiety in Academic Settings / Anticipatory Anxiety / Social Anxiety in School / Academic Performance Anxiety?

acceptedAnswer.text: School anxiety encompasses a range of anxiety-related difficulties that interfere with school attendance, participation, or wellbeing. It may present as separation anxiety (fear of being away from parents/home), social anxiety (fear of peer judgment, social situations), academic anxiety (fear of failure, tests, performance), or generalized anxiety that attaches to the school environment. School refusal behavior — difficulty attending school associated with emotional distress — exists on a spectrum from morning resistance with eventual attendance to complete school avoidance. Key features include anticipatory anxiety (distress about upcoming school before it happens), physical symptoms (stomachaches, headaches, nausea with no medical cause), and the paradox that anxiety about school often exceeds actual distress during school. Without intervention, school anxiety can escalate, leading to chronic absenteeism, academic difficulties, social isolation, and reinforcement of avoidance patterns. Evidence-based intervention involves graduated exposure, coping skill development, parent coaching, school collaboration, and sometimes professional mental health support. Common signs include: Crying, pleading, or refusing to get ready for school, Physical complaints (stomachaches, headaches, nausea) that appear primarily on school days, Sleep disturbances — difficulty falling asleep Sunday nights, nightmares about school, early waking, Excessive questioning about the upcoming school day, Difficulty separating from parent at drop-off.

mainEntity.name: How does a Transitional Objects and Parent Connection Items help with school anxiety?

acceptedAnswer.text: Separation anxiety at its core is fear of disconnection — the child's nervous system doesn't believe they'll be safe apart from their attachment figure. Transitional objects bridge that gap. A small item from home — a parent's scarf sprayed with their perfume, a family photo, a keychain the parent has 'charged with love,' a heart drawn on the child's wrist and matching one on the parent's — provides a tangible reminder that the connection continues even when apart. When anxiety rises during the school day, the child can touch, hold, or look at the transitional object and activate the felt sense of parental presence. For younger children, the object may be visible (small stuffed animal in backpack). For older children, subtle items work better (parent's button in pocket, shared bracelet, note in lunchbox). The object isn't magic — it's a regulation anchor that helps the child access their calm state when the parent isn't physically present. Price: ₹0–500. DIY option: Use existing meaningful items. Parent can 'charge' an object with love through a ritual (holding it together, saying special words). Draw matching symbols on wrists each morning. Write notes for lunchbox discovery. Give child something that smells like home. Create a 'bravery stone' they carry in pocket. Safety: Ensure items are school-appropriate and won't be confiscated. Check school policies on personal items. For younger children, attach items securely to prevent loss. Have backup items for when primary object is forgotten.

mainEntity.name: How does a Visual Morning Routine Schedules help with school anxiety?

acceptedAnswer.text: Anxious mornings are chaotic mornings, and chaos feeds anxiety. When every step requires a decision, a negotiation, or a reminder, cognitive load depletes the child's already-limited coping resources. Visual morning routine schedules externalize the sequence, removing decisions and reducing parent-child conflict. The schedule shows what happens and in what order: wake up, bathroom, get dressed, eat breakfast, brush teeth, pack backpack, put on shoes, get in car. The child can see what's completed and what's coming. There's no arguing with the schedule — it's not the parent's arbitrary demand, it's just what comes next. For anxious children, predictability is regulating. Knowing exactly what will happen reduces the 'what ifs' that fuel anticipatory anxiety. The schedule also builds in anxiety-management steps: 'Take three deep breaths' can be a visual step between 'put on shoes' and 'get in car.' The routine becomes a container for the morning, making it feel manageable rather than endless. Price: ₹200–800. DIY option: Create a simple picture schedule with photos of your child doing each step. Use velcro for moveable 'done' markers. Include both routine steps and coping steps. Post at child's eye level. Review schedule the night before so morning has no surprises. Safety: Introduce schedules during calm times, not during crisis. Practice the routine on low-stress days (weekends) before using for school mornings. Keep schedule consistent — changes to routine can increase anxiety.

mainEntity.name: How does a Worry Journals and Worry Time Containers help with school anxiety?

acceptedAnswer.text: Anxious children often have brains that won't stop producing 'what if' thoughts. These worries feel urgent, demanding immediate attention, and they multiply when pushed away. Worry journals and worry time containers don't eliminate worries — they defer them. The child writes (or draws, for younger children) their worries in a dedicated journal or on slips of paper placed in a 'worry box.' The deal: worries are acknowledged and saved, not dismissed. But they're dealt with during designated 'worry time' — a specific 15-minute period (not at bedtime, not before school) when worries are reviewed, discussed, and addressed. This teaches the brain that worries will be heard, just not right now. Over time, children often find that worries written in the morning feel smaller by worry time. The act of externalizing the worry — getting it out of the head and onto paper — provides immediate relief and creates distance from the anxious thought. Price: ₹100–400. DIY option: Any small notebook works. Decorate it as the official 'Worry Journal.' Create a worry box from any container for worry slips. Establish worry time ritual — same time, same place, with full parental attention. Important: actually hold worry time. If you skip it, the system loses credibility. Safety: Worry journals work for typical anxiety. If worries are about harm to self or others, trauma content, or severe intrusive thoughts, professional mental health support is needed beyond this strategy.

mainEntity.name: How does a Calm-Down Cards and Coping Strategy Rings help with school anxiety?

acceptedAnswer.text: When anxiety floods the nervous system, the thinking brain goes offline. Children (and adults) forget their coping strategies precisely when they need them most. Calm-down cards and coping strategy rings put strategies in visual, tangible form that's accessible during distress. A ring of cards (on a binder ring, attached to backpack) or a folding card set contains the child's proven coping strategies: 'Take 5 deep breaths,' 'Squeeze my stress ball,' 'Think of my happy place,' 'Look at my family photo,' 'Count backwards from 10.' When anxiety rises, the child can flip through cards and find a strategy that feels doable in that moment. The cards work because they externalize memory — the child doesn't have to remember strategies, just look at them. Cards can be customized to the specific child with strategies they've practiced and found effective. Having cards also builds agency — the child has tools, visible and portable, that they control. Price: ₹150–600. DIY option: Create cards from index cards or cardstock. Include simple pictures with words. Punch hole, attach to binder ring. Let child help choose which strategies to include. Practice each strategy when calm so it's familiar during stress. Laminate for durability. Safety: Cards are reminders, not magic. Strategies must be taught and practiced when calm before they'll be accessible during anxiety. Cards work best as part of a broader coping skill-building approach.

mainEntity.name: How does a Fidgets and Sensory Regulation Tools help with school anxiety?

acceptedAnswer.text: Anxiety is a whole-body experience. The nervous system activates, muscles tense, energy builds with nowhere to go. Fidgets and sensory tools give that anxious energy an outlet, channeling it through the hands and body rather than letting it spiral into panic. Quiet, classroom-appropriate fidgets — stress balls, textured putty, fidget cubes, smooth stones, tangle toys — provide tactile input that's inherently regulating. The repetitive motion of fidgeting activates the parasympathetic nervous system, promoting calm. For anxious children, having a fidget in the pocket or desk means they always have a subtle way to self-regulate without disrupting class or drawing attention. The fidget becomes a regulation anchor — something to reach for when 'what ifs' start building. Research supports that fidgeting can improve focus and reduce anxiety, particularly for children with attentional or anxiety differences. Price: ₹100–800. DIY option: DIY options: smooth stones, fabric scraps to rub, hair ties on wrist to snap gently, homemade stress balls (balloon filled with flour). The best fidget is one the child finds genuinely regulating — let them test options. Safety: All fidgets must be quiet and non-distracting for classroom use. Check with teacher first. Avoid fidgets that make noise, light up, or become toys rather than tools. If fidgets become distracting rather than regulating, reassess.

mainEntity.name: How does a Social Stories and School Preparation Books help with school anxiety?

acceptedAnswer.text: Anticipatory anxiety feeds on uncertainty. The anxious brain generates 'what if' scenarios, most of them negative, and can't distinguish imagined disasters from likely reality. Social stories and school preparation books replace scary unknowns with concrete, manageable knowledge. A social story walks through the school day in specific, reassuring detail: 'I will get to school. I will hang up my backpack. I will sit at my desk. Mrs. Johnson will say good morning. We will do math first. If I feel worried, I can use my coping tools. Mom will pick me up at 3:00.' The story can address specific fears: what happens if I need the bathroom, what if I don't understand something, what if someone is mean to me. By previewing experiences and embedding coping statements, social stories reduce the power of 'what ifs' with 'I knows.' Reading the story the night before and morning of school primes the brain for what will happen and builds confidence through repetition. Price: ₹200–800. DIY option: Create personalized social stories using photos of your child's actual school, classroom, and teacher. Write in first person ('I will...'). Include coping statements ('If I feel worried, I can...'). Read repeatedly until content feels familiar and boring — that's the goal. Safety: Social stories should be realistic, not falsely reassuring. Acknowledge that hard things might happen and include coping responses. Avoid promising things you can't guarantee ('Nothing bad will happen').

@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/emotional regulation / anxiety management / school participation / behavioral support/school anxiety / school refusal / separation anxiety / academic anxiety / social anxiety in school settings / morning transition support/9-materials-that-help-with-school-anxiety#howto
@graph.name: How to Help a Child with School Anxiety
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. School anxiety is not a choice, not manipulation, and not a discipline problem. It's an anxiety disorder manifesting around school triggers. The child's nervous system perceives threat, activates fight-or-flight, and generates genuine physical symptoms and emotional distress. Treatment requires validating the reality of the experience while gradually building the child's capacity to tolerate school despite anxiety. Avoidance provides short-term relief but long-term escalation. The goal isn't eliminating anxiety (often impossible) but helping children function with anxiety, building evidence that they can handle hard things.

Key features include anticipatory anxiety (distress about upcoming school before it happens), physical symptoms (stomachaches, headaches, nausea with no medical cause), and the paradox that anxiety about school often exceeds actual distress during school. Without intervention, school anxiety can escalate, leading to chronic absenteeism, academic difficulties, social isolation, and reinforcement of avoidance patterns. Evidence-based intervention involves graduated exposure, coping skill development, parent coaching, school collaboration, and sometimes professional mental health support. signOrSymptom.name: Crying, pleading, or refusing to get ready for school
Physical complaints (stomachaches, headaches, nausea) that appear primarily on school days

Excessive questioning about the upcoming school day

citation.name: Research on School Anxiety and Evidence-Based Interventions
citation.description: Research indicates that cognitive-behavioral approaches, graduated exposure, parent involvement, and school collaboration are effective interventions for school anxiety and school refusal. Transitional objects, coping skill development, visual supports, and structured morning routines can reduce anxiety symptoms and improve school attendance. Early intervention prevents escalation of avoidance patterns.

When every step requires a decision, a negotiation, or a reminder, cognitive load depletes the child's already-limited coping resources. Visual morning routine schedules externalize the sequence, removing decisions and reducing parent-child conflict. The schedule shows what happens and in what order: wake up, bathroom, get dressed, eat breakfast, brush teeth, pack backpack, put on shoes, get in car. The child can see what's completed and what's coming. There's no arguing with the schedule — it's not the parent's arbitrary demand, it's just what comes next. For anxious children, predictability is regulating. Knowing exactly what will happen reduces the 'what ifs' that fuel anticipatory anxiety. The schedule also builds in anxiety-management steps: 'Take three deep breaths' can be a visual step between 'put on shoes' and 'get in car.' The routine becomes a container for the morning, making it feel manageable rather than endless.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a simple picture schedule with photos of your child doing each step. Use velcro for moveable 'done' markers. Include both routine steps and coping steps. Post at child's eye level. Review schedule the night before so morning has no surprises.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Introduce schedules during calm times, not during crisis. Practice the routine on low-stress days (weekends) before using for school mornings. Keep schedule consistent — changes to routine can increase anxiety.

item.name: Worry Journals and Worry Time Containers
item.description: Anxious children often have brains that won't stop producing 'what if' thoughts. These worries feel urgent, demanding immediate attention, and they multiply when pushed away. Worry journals and worry time containers don't eliminate worries — they defer them. The child writes (or draws, for younger children) their worries in a dedicated journal or on slips of paper placed in a 'worry box.' The deal: worries are acknowledged and saved, not dismissed. But they're dealt with during designated 'worry time' — a specific 15-minute period (not at bedtime, not before school) when worries are reviewed, discussed, and addressed. This teaches the brain that worries will be heard, just not right now. Over time, children often find that worries written in the morning feel smaller by worry time. The act of externalizing the worry — getting it out of the head and onto paper — provides immediate relief and creates distance from the anxious thought.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Any small notebook works. Decorate it as the official 'Worry Journal.' Create a worry box from any container for worry slips. Establish worry time ritual — same time, same place, with full parental attention. Important: actually hold worry time. If you skip it, the system loses credibility.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Worry journals work for typical anxiety. If worries are about harm to self or others, trauma content, or severe intrusive thoughts, professional mental health support is needed beyond this strategy.

item.name: Calm-Down Cards and Coping Strategy Rings
item.description: When anxiety floods the nervous system, the thinking brain goes offline. Children (and adults) forget their coping strategies precisely when they need them most. Calm-down cards and coping strategy rings put strategies in visual, tangible form that's accessible during distress. A ring of cards (on a binder ring, attached to backpack) or a folding card set contains the child's proven coping strategies: 'Take 5 deep breaths,' 'Squeeze my stress ball,' 'Think of my happy place,' 'Look at my family photo,' 'Count backwards from 10.' When anxiety rises, the child can flip through cards and find a strategy that feels doable in that moment. The cards work because they externalize memory — the child doesn't have to remember strategies, just look at them. Cards can be customized to the specific child with strategies they've practiced and found effective. Having cards also builds agency — the child has tools, visible and portable, that they control.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create cards from index cards or cardstock. Include simple pictures with words. Punch hole, attach to binder ring. Let child help choose which strategies to include. Practice each strategy when calm so it's familiar during stress. Laminate for durability.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Cards are reminders, not magic. Strategies must be taught and practiced when calm before they'll be accessible during anxiety. Cards work best as part of a broader coping skill-building approach.

item.name: Fidgets and Sensory Regulation Tools
item.description: Anxiety is a whole-body experience. The nervous system activates, muscles tense, energy builds with nowhere to go. Fidgets and sensory tools give that anxious energy an outlet, channeling it through the hands and body rather than letting it spiral into panic. Quiet, classroom-appropriate fidgets — stress balls, textured putty, fidget cubes, smooth stones, tangle toys — provide tactile input that's inherently regulating. The repetitive motion of fidgeting activates the parasympathetic nervous system, promoting calm. For anxious children, having a fidget in the pocket or desk means they always have a subtle way to self-regulate without disrupting class or drawing attention. The fidget becomes a regulation anchor — something to reach for when 'what ifs' start building. Research supports that fidgeting can improve focus and reduce anxiety, particularly for children with attentional or anxiety differences.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: DIY options: smooth stones, fabric scraps to rub, hair ties on wrist to snap gently, homemade stress balls (balloon filled with flour). The best fidget is one the child finds genuinely regulating — let them test options.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: All fidgets must be quiet and non-distracting for classroom use. Check with teacher first. Avoid fidgets that make noise, light up, or become toys rather than tools. If fidgets become distracting rather than regulating, reassess.

item.name: Social Stories and School Preparation Books
item.description: Anticipatory anxiety feeds on uncertainty. The anxious brain generates 'what if' scenarios, most of them negative, and can't distinguish imagined disasters from likely reality. Social stories and school preparation books replace scary unknowns with concrete, manageable knowledge. A social story walks through the school day in specific, reassuring detail: 'I will get to school. I will hang up my backpack. I will sit at my desk. Mrs. Johnson will say good morning. We will do math first. If I feel worried, I can use my coping tools. Mom will pick me up at 3:00.' The story can address specific fears: what happens if I need the bathroom, what if I don't understand something, what if someone is mean to me. By previewing experiences and embedding coping statements, social stories reduce the power of 'what ifs' with 'I knows.' Reading the story the night before and morning of school primes the brain for what will happen and builds confidence through repetition.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create personalized social stories using photos of your child's actual school, classroom, and teacher. Write in first person ('I will...'). Include coping statements ('If I feel worried, I can...'). Read repeatedly until content feels familiar and boring — that's the goal.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Social stories should be realistic, not falsely reassuring. Acknowledge that hard things might happen and include coping responses. Avoid promising things you can't guarantee ('Nothing bad will happen').

item.name: Breathing and Grounding Tool Cards
item.description: Deep breathing and grounding techniques work — they activate the parasympathetic nervous system and bring the body out of fight-or-flight. But anxious children (and adults) often struggle to remember or initiate these techniques during acute anxiety. Breathing and grounding tool cards make these strategies concrete and portable. A card showing the '5-4-3-2-1' grounding technique (5 things you see, 4 you hear, 3 you feel, 2 you smell, 1 you taste) stays in the child's pocket. A visual showing 'smell the flower, blow out the candle' breathing sits in their desk. When anxiety rises, the card provides both a reminder and a guide. Visual breathing patterns — tracing a shape, watching an expanding circle — are particularly helpful because they occupy the visual channel while regulating the breath. Cards also give children agency: instead of relying on an adult to coach them through calming, they can self-initiate using their tools.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create simple cards with visual breathing patterns: trace the square (breathe in on one side, hold on next, out on third, hold on fourth), lazy 8 breathing, star breathing (breathe in at one point, out moving to next). Include 5-4-3-2-1 grounding on a card. Practice techniques when calm so they're familiar.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Some children hyperventilate when told to 'take deep breaths' during acute panic. If this happens, focus on slowing the exhale rather than deep inhale, or use grounding techniques instead of breathing focus.

item.name: Brave Charts and Graduated Exposure Trackers
item.description: Overcoming anxiety requires doing the scary thing — repeatedly — until the brain learns it's survivable. But anxious children feel like they're failing every day. Brave charts and exposure trackers reframe the narrative: each time you do something hard, you're building bravery. A brave chart tracks 'brave actions' — getting in the car without tears, walking into school, staying all day, trying something scary at recess. Points, stickers, or progress markers accumulate, showing evidence of courage that anxiety wants the child to ignore. Graduated exposure trackers help break big fears into smaller steps, celebrating progress at each level: 'Week 1: Mom walks me to the classroom door. Week 2: Mom walks me to the school entrance. Week 3: Mom drops me at the curb.' The visual progress provides motivation and counteracts the anxious narrative that 'I can't do this.' Evidence of bravery, accumulated over time, builds genuine confidence.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a simple chart with the child. Define 'brave actions' together — the bar should be achievable. Stickers, checkmarks, or drawing progress all work. Focus on attempts, not outcomes. Celebrate the effort of showing up anxious and doing it anyway.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Brave charts should never shame anxiety. The goal is to highlight courage that exists, not create pressure that increases anxiety. 'You were so brave even though you cried' validates both the fear and the effort.

item.name: Check-In Systems and School-Home Communication Tools
item.description: Anxious children often worry about not having access to parents during the school day. 'What if I need you and I can't reach you?' Check-in systems provide structured, predictable parent contact that satisfies the need for connection without enabling excessive reassurance-seeking. A scheduled check-in might be a brief call at lunchtime, a text exchange at a set time, or a visual signal system with the teacher. The child knows exactly when contact will happen and can hold on until then. For younger children, a 'mommy and me' notebook that travels between home and school allows written communication — the child can write or draw what's happening, and the parent responds. The key is predictability and limits: the check-in happens at the scheduled time, not on demand. This teaches the child they can tolerate the gap between check-ins while ensuring they're not completely cut off. Over time, check-ins can be gradually reduced as the child's tolerance builds.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Establish a predictable check-in time that works for parent and school. Start with more frequent contact if needed, then gradually space out. Create a school-home notebook for younger children. Use visual countdown to check-in time. Keep check-ins brief and positive, not focused on detailed anxiety discussion.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Check-ins should decrease over time, not become permanent accommodations that prevent independence. The goal is bridging toward independence, not maintaining dependence. If check-ins are increasing rather than decreasing, reassess approach.

socialMedia.caption: Every morning is a battle. The tears start before sunrise. The stomachaches have no medical cause. The drop-off takes twenty minutes and leaves you both shattered. Here are 9 materials that help with school anxiety: transitional objects, visual morning schedules, worry journals, coping strategy cards, fidgets, social stories, breathing cards, brave charts, and check-in systems. These tools don't cure anxiety — they make the journey through it survivable. Your child is braver than they know. Save for hard mornings! 🔖 #SchoolAnxiety #AnxiousKids #PinnacleBlooms
